items.question: Who benefits from a chart-type decision framework in ML projects?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, and analysts who produce notebook EDA and stakeholder reports benefit—choosing bar, line, scatter, histogram, box, or heatmap before touching Matplotlib, Seaborn, or Plotly prevents misleading visuals and speeds exploration.

items.question: What is the first step when choosing a chart type for a dataset column or metric?
items.answer: State the analytical question in plain language—compare categories, show one variable's distribution, relate two numerics, track a metric over ordered steps, compare numeric spread across groups, or summarize a matrix—then map that question to one of the six core chart families.

items.question: When should you use a line chart instead of a bar chart?
items.answer: Use a line chart when the x-axis has meaningful order—time, epoch, training step—and you want to show how a numeric metric changes along that sequence. Use a bar chart when x categories are unordered labels or you compare discrete counts.

items.question: Where does choosing chart types sit relative to Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly?
items.answer: This topic sits upstream: it decides bar vs scatter vs heatmap from the question and variable types. Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly sit downstream as implementation layers that draw the chosen family with different syntax and interactivity.

items.question: Why is a histogram the default for inspecting one numeric feature before modeling?
items.answer: A histogram reveals distribution shape—skew, modality, outliers, and range—which drives scaler choice, transformation, and distributional assumptions linked to Common distributions. No other single chart answers univariate numeric shape as directly.

items.question: How do you decide between scatter and heatmap for multivariate numeric data?
items.answer: Use a heatmap when the data is already a square matrix such as df.corr() and you need an overview of many pairwise strengths at once. Use scatter when you are investigating one specific relationship between two numeric columns in detail, especially for nonlinear patterns.

items.question: Bar chart vs box plot: when does each win for ML reporting?
items.answer: Bar charts compare single aggregated values per category—counts, mean accuracy, total revenue. Box plots compare full numeric distributions per category—median, quartiles, outliers—when spread and skew across groups matter more than one number per bar.

items.question: Histogram vs box plot for univariate numeric EDA?
items.answer: Histograms show full distribution shape including multimodality and skew—best for one variable in depth. Box plots compress to five-number summary—best when comparing the same numeric variable across many categories on one compact panel.

items.question: Scatter vs line for two numeric columns in a DataFrame?
items.answer: Scatter treats both axes as meaningful numeric variables and reveals correlation, clusters, and outliers. Line connects points in row order and implies progression—appropriate only when the x-axis is intentionally ordered (e.g. epoch), not for arbitrary feature pairs.

items.question: Cause: line chart used for model names on the x-axis in arbitrary training order. Effect?
items.answer: Readers infer a false temporal or ranked progression between models; reported 'trends' between bars are meaningless. Switch to a bar chart of the metric per model so comparison is length-based, not path-based.

items.question: Cause: skipping histograms and relying only on mean and standard deviation for feature scaling. Effect?
items.answer: Heavy skew, zero inflation, or bimodality stay hidden; StandardScaler assumptions fail silently and model performance or convergence suffers. Histograms surface shapes that trigger log transforms or robust scaling.

items.question: What if you plot 500,000 training points as a solid opaque scatter?
items.answer: Overplotting saturates the panel into an unreadable blob; apparent density misleads EDA. Subsample for overview, lower alpha, or use a density implementation—but keep the chart family as scatter because the question is still bivariate numeric relationship.
